source_metadata.title_raw: Julia M. Lew - A-18-149 - October 15, 2018 - Visalia

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Julia M. Lew, Esq. 
City Attorney 
McCormick Kabat Jenner & Lew 
4010 South Demaree Street 
Visalia, CA 93277 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-149 
Dear Ms. Lew: 
October 15, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the City of Porterville regarding 
the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act ("Act") and Government Code 
Section 1090 on behalf of Porterville Administrative Services Director Patrice Hildreth. 1 Please 
note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and 
Section 1090, not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions, such as common law conflict 
of interest or the Public Contract Code. 
Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71); any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not 
the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
Regarding our advice on Section 1090, we are required to forward your request and all 
pertinent facts relating to the request to the Attorney General's Office and the Tulare County 
District Attorney's Office, which we have done. (Section 1097.l(c)(3).) We did not receive a 
written response from either entity. (Section 1097.l(c)(4).) We are also required to advise you that, 
for purposes of Section 1090, the following advice "is not admissible in a criminal proceeding 
against any individual other than the requestor." (See Section 1097.l(c)(S).) 
QUESTION 
Would the promotion of Porterville Administrative Services Director Patrice Hildreth to 
Deputy City Manager create a disqualifying conflict of interest under Section 1090 or the Act due 
to her spouse's current employment by the City of Porterville Fire Department? 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


CONCLUSION 
File No. A-18-149 
Page No. 2 
Promotion to Deputy City Manager alone would neither create a disqualifying conflict of 
interest under the Act or Section 1090. However, Section 1090 and the Act prohibits Director 
Hildreth from taking part in future decisions involving her spouse's terms of employment or 
amending his employment contract. To the extent that Director Hildreth is involved in a decision 
that implicates her spouse or his salary, she should seek further advice. 
FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
Porterville has a population of approximately 60,000. The City implements a typical 
"council-manager" form of government, where the City Manager (via input from department heads 
and other City staff, including the Deputy City Manager) makes recommendations to the City 
Council. In turn, the City Council considers input from the City Manager and department head staff 
before making decisions. The City Manager also runs and oversees the day-to-day operations of the 
City. 
The City Manager is the administrative head of the City government. Per City Charter, he or 
she is responsible for all appointments and removals of department heads, subordinate officers, and 
employees. Additionally, the City Manager has general supervision and control over all employees. 
The City Manager also has the power to examine the conduct of any officers or employees of the 
City. The City Council is responsible for fixing the compensation of certain appointed officers and 
employees, but the City Council has authorized the City Manager to hire and set employees' 
compensation as may be necessary. 
Administrative Services Director Patrice Hildreth's spouse, Michael Hildreth, is a Captain 
with the City of Porterville's Fire Department. Captain positions are not considered "department 
heads." They are not part of the "command staff' of the Department. Instead, they report to 
Battalion Chiefs, who in tum report to the Fire Chief. Captain Hildreth has been a full-time 
employee of the Department since 1999, predating Director Hildreth's employment with the City by 
greater than one year. 
Disciplinary appeals of Fire Department employees are made before a three-member hearing 
panel consisting of one City employee selected by the appellant, one·panel member selected by the 
City, and the third panel member selected from a list of five neutral hearing officers jointly 
developed by the City and the bargaining unit. The decision of the hearing panel is final and 
binding on all parties. 
Regarding the Fire Officer Series specifically, of which Captain Hildreth is a member, there 
are two administrative hearing processes depending on the severity of the discipline. The first 
process provides that the City Manager or his designee serves as the hearing officer, solely 
responsible for disciplinary action. If he or she cannot serve because of actual bias, prejudice, or 
interest, a mediator from California State Mediation and Conciliation Service serves. The presiding 
officer's decision is final and binding. Under the second process, the disciplinary action is reviewed 
by a hearing panel, consisting of a city employee selected by the appellant, a city employee selected 
by the employee's department head, and a third member ("Hearing Officer") selected from a list 


File No. A-18-149 
Page No. 3 
developed by the City and the bargaining unit. The City Manager's only responsibility under this 
second process is to appoint an attorney to advise the Hearing Officer. 
The City Manager would like to consider promoting Director Hildreth to the position of 
Deputy City Manager, an at-will employment position. The Deputy City Manager position would 
continue to be responsible for the City Administrative Services, but in addition Director Hildreth 
would be responsible for all City Manager activities when the City Manager is unavailable or as he 
designates. 
One of the Deputy City Manager responsibilities is to serve as the City's Hearing Officer on 
labor and employment issues; however, in some cases this responsibility would be superseded by 
the terms of employee-organization memos of understanding. Additionally, she would be 
responsible for all City Manager responsibilities in the event of the City Manager's absence or 
unavailability. 
Director Hildreth would like to ensure that her appointment to the Deputy City Manager 
position would not create an impermissible conflict of interest due to her husband's current position 
with the City. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 1090: 
Section 1090 generally prohibits public officers, while acting in their official capacities, 
from making contracts in which they are financially interested. Under Section 1090, "the prohibited 
act is the making of a contract in which the official has a financial interest" (People v. Honig (1996) 
48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333), and officials are deemed to have a financial interest in a contract if they 
might profit from it in any way. (Ibid.) When the Section 1090 prohibition is applicable, the 
prohibition acts as an absolute bar to the officer's governing body or board entering into the 
prohibited contract. (69 Ops.Cal.Atty.Gen. 102 (1986).) 
Director Hildreth has a financial interest in her spouse's salary and her employment contract 
with the City. Accordingly, Section 1090 prohibits her from making recommendations to the City 
Council concerning any future contracts implicating her spouse, such as changes in his terms of 
employment under the existing contract or the renewal of the existing contract unless an exception 
to Section 1090 applies.2 
2 An official has an interest in the community and separate property income of his or her spouse. (Thorpe v. 
Long Beach Community College Dist. (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 655.) Thus, a member of a board or commission is 
financially interested in his or her spouse's source of income for purposes of Section 1090. This is true even if the 
husband and wife have an agreement that their own earnings are to be treated as separate property, since each spouse is 
liable for the necessities oflife for the other. (Reece v. Alcoholic Bev. Etc. Appeals Bd (1976) 64 Cal.App.3d 675, 
683).) 


Spousal Noninterest Exception. 
File No. A-18-149 
Page No. 4 
Under Section 1091.5, an official has a noninterest in his or her spouse's employment or 
holding office if it has existed for at least one year prior to the official's election or appointment to 
office. (Section 1091.5(a)(6).) Captain Hildreth has been a full-time employee of the Department 
since 1999, predating Director Hildreth's employment with the City by more than one year. 
However, this exception is narrowly construed. The exception permits a spouse to retain his or her 
position, even though the other spouse was a member of the board and participated in the 
employment contract, if the terms of employment do not change (Thorpe v. Long Beach Community 
College Dist. (2000) 83 Cal.App.4th 655.) The exception does not permit a spouse to participate in 
a contract that would give the other spouse a promotion or similar change in status. 
The Act: 
Under Sections 87100 and 87103, a conflict of interest exists whenever a public official 
makes, participates in making, or uses his or her position to influence a governmental decision 
which has a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on one or more of the official's 
financial interests. Interests from which a disqualifying conflict of interest may arise are 
enumerated in Section 87103. Based upon the facts provided, the only interest implicated is 
Director Hildreth' s interest in her personal finances. 3 
An official always has an interest in his or her "personal finances, including those of his or 
her immediate family. This is referred to as the "personal financial effects" rule. A decision has a 
personal financial effect on a public official if the decision will result in the personal expenses, 
income, assets, or liabilities of the official (or the official's immediate family) increasing or 
decreasing. (Section 87103.) Generally, this prohibits an official from taking part in a decision to 
appoint, hire, fire, promote, demote, or suspend without pay or take disciplinary action against a 
spouse, or to set a spouse's salary different from salaries paid to employees in the same 
classification or position. (Regulation 18704(d)(3).) An official may take part in a decision affecting 
the salary or benefits of his or her spouse only if the decision applies equally to all employees in the 
same bargaining unit or representative group. (See Regulation 18702.5(b)(l).) 
To the extent that any future decision implicates Captain Hildreth or his salary, Director 
Hildreth must be aware that she may be disqualified from taking part in the decisions under Section 
1090 or the Act. She may wish to seek additional advice prior to taking part in any decision that 
implicates her spouse or his salary. 
3 Under Section 87103(c), an official has a financial interest in "(a]ny source of income ... aggregating five 
hundred dollars ($500) or more in value provided or promised to, received by, the public official within 12 months prior 
to the time when the decision is made." Income includes an official's community property interest in his or her spouse's 
income. However, "salary and reimbursement for expenses" received from a local government agency are not "income" 
for purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(b)(2).) 


File No. A-18-149 
Page No. 5 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
By: 
RPOC:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
~ ?oh 
~. O'Connor 
Counsel, Legal Division
